Hydrofluoric acid (HF) differs fundamentally from other strong acids because instead of immediately denaturing proteins and inducing coagulation necrosis, it acts by disrupting intracellular calcium levels through the formation of insoluble calcium fluoride complexes—an indirect pathological process that prevents early protein coagulation. This makes HF the correct choice among listed options.
